title: How to Create a UI/UX Skill for Claude (Anthropic Agent Skills)

content.text: A well-built UI/UX skill lets Claude consistently follow your design standards and workflow, without you re-explaining your process every time. Think of it as packaging your design “playbook” into a reusable instruction set that Claude can load automatically when a request matches your triggers.
This post walks through how to create a practical UI/UX skill from scratch: structure, frontmatter, triggers, instructions, testing, and iteration.
1) What “a skill” is (and why it matters)
An Anthropic skill is a folder that teaches Claude how to handle a repeatable workflow. At minimum, it contains one required file:
SKILL.md (required): Markdown instructions with YAML frontmatter
scripts/ (optional): Python/Bash utilities for validation or generation
references/ (optional): extra docs Claude can open only when needed
assets/ (optional): templates, sample output, brand tokens, etc.
The power comes from progressive disclosure:
Frontmatter: always loaded, used for deciding when to use the skill
SKILL.md body: loaded only when the skill is relevant
Bundled references/assets: loaded only if Claude chooses to open them
This keeps context light while still giving Claude strong “specialist” behavior.
2) Pick 2–3 concrete UI/UX use cases (start small)
Before writing anything, define what the skill should reliably do.
Here are strong starter use cases for a UI/UX skill:
Use case A: Turn product requirements into a UX flow
Trigger phrases: “design a flow”, “UX flow”, “user journey”, “happy path”, “edge cases”
Result: A structured flow, edge cases, states, error handling, and acceptance criteria.
Use case B: Accessibility-first UI review
Trigger phrases: “accessibility audit”, “WCAG”, “contrast”, “focus state”, “keyboard navigation”
Result: A prioritized issues list + fixes + test checklist.
Use case C: Design critique in a consistent format
Trigger phrases: “critique this design”, “UX feedback”, “review my UI”
Result: Feedback organized by severity, user impact, and next steps.
Pick the ones you actually repeat weekly. You can expand later.
3) Create the skill folder (follow the rules exactly)
Folder name must be kebab-case (lowercase, hyphens):
Inside it, create:
Critical rules:
File must be named exactly SKILL.md (case-sensitive).
Skill name must be kebab-case, no spaces, no capitals.
Do not include README.md inside the skill folder.
Do not put XML angle brackets < > in the frontmatter.
Avoid using “claude” or “anthropic” in the skill name (reserved patterns). You can mention them in the body text.
4) Write strong YAML frontmatter (this controls when it triggers)
Your frontmatter must answer:
1) What it does
2) When to use it (trigger phrases)
Here’s a good frontmatter for a UI/UX skill:
Tips:
Be specific. “Helps with design” is too vague.
Include the phrases people actually type.
To prevent over-triggering, add boundaries in the description (for example: “Do NOT use for branding”).
5) Write the SKILL.md body (your UI/UX workflow)
Below the frontmatter, write clear, actionable steps Claude can follow every time.
Here’s a solid starter template you can copy into SKILL.md:
6) Add optional references (make it smarter without bloating it)
If you have a personal style guide or recurring patterns (like destructive actions, form validation rules, or UI writing guidance), put them into:
Then in SKILL.md, tell Claude when to consult them, for example:
“If the flow includes deletion, consult references/destructive-actions-pattern.md.”
7) Testing: make sure it triggers and stays consistent
Create a mini test set (10–20 prompts).
Should trigger
“Create a UX flow for deleting an account safely.”
“Do an accessibility audit of this settings screen.”
“Write UI specs for a password reset screen with error states.”
Should not trigger
“Write a LinkedIn post about design.”
“What’s the weather?”
“Help me debug Python” (unless your skill is meant to cover it)
Then iterate:
If it doesn’t trigger: add clearer trigger phrases to description.
If it triggers too often: narrow scope and add “Do NOT use when…”.
8) Distribution tips (so you can reuse it anywhere)
For personal use:
Zip the folder and upload it in Claude’s Skills UI (or place it in your local skills directory).
For sharing:
Host the folder in a GitHub repo.
Put human-facing documentation in the repo-level README (not inside the skill folder).
Include example prompts and screenshots.
Example starter prompts to include
“Turn this PRD into a UX flow with edge cases and acceptance criteria.”
“Critique this onboarding screen with severity and suggested fixes.”
“Design an accessible confirmation modal for destructive actions.”
“Create a UI spec for a form: labels, validations, error messages, and states.”
Closing
Start with one workflow you repeatedly do (like accessibility-first flow design). Write the skill so the output is always structured, testable, accessible, and consistent. Then expand it over time.
The Challenge
Destructive actions (e.g., deleting accounts or data) are irreversible, yet many interfaces design them poorly—leading to accidental data loss, frustrated users, and eroded trust.
Key Problems Identified
Poorly designed dialog: Unclear "Proceed" button, no visible cancel option
Example of problematic design: Ambiguous labels, low contrast, and missing safeguards.
Ambiguous labels ("Proceed" instead of "Delete Account").
No confirmation dialogs, allowing irreversible actions to trigger instantly.
Accessibility failures: Low contrast, small click targets, missing focus states.
Inconsistent patterns across platforms (desktop vs. mobile).
Goals
Prevent accidental destructive actions.
Ensure destructive buttons are fully accessible (WCAG 2.1 Level AA compliant).
Create a consistent, predictable pattern across all products.
Educate internal teams on the importance of accessible destructive design.
Approach
🔍 User Testing & Heuristic Analysis
We conducted usability testing and heuristic evaluations on key interfaces (e.g., account settings, file managers). Key findings included:
Users skipped warning text due to poor contrast or vague language.
Keyboard users struggled to locate the active button due to missing focus indicators.
On mobile, destructive buttons were too close to primary actions like “Save.”
Solutions & Design Decisions
Clear, Action-Oriented Labels
Problem: Vague labels like “Proceed” confused users.
Solution:
We replaced ambiguous labels with explicit action verbs, such as:
“Delete My Account”
“Remove User”
“Cancel Subscription”
This increased user understanding and reduced hesitation during task completion.
✅ 2. Confirmation Dialogs
Problem: Actions occurred immediately without warnings.
Solution:
We introduced confirmation modals that included:
Clear warning messages about consequences
Optional checkboxes for sensitive deletions
Clear button options: “Cancel” (non-destructive), “Confirm Delete” (destructive)
✅ 3. Visual Differentiation
Problem: Destructive buttons visually blended with other controls.
Solution:
Used high-contrast red color with text + icon (e.g., 🗑️ DELETE)
Avoided placing destructive actions next to primary buttons
Styled destructive actions as secondary, except within confirmation dialogs
✅ 4. Accessibility Compliance
Problem: Buttons failed to meet basic WCAG standards.
Solution:
We updated destructive buttons to meet:
Target size of at least 24×24px (WCAG 2.5.8)
Contrast ratio ≥ 4.5:1 between text and background (WCAG 1.4.3)
Focus visibility (WCAG 2.4.7) with clear outlines and spacing
We also tested with assistive technologies to ensure compatibility.
Results
92% reduction in support tickets related to accidental deletion.
Improved task completion rates in usability tests.
Positive qualitative feedback from users: “I feel much safer using this now.”
Design system updated with clear patterns for destructive actions, adopted across multiple teams.
Key Takeaways
Destructive buttons must be designed with caution: clear labels, confirmation dialogs, and accessible styles are non-negotiable.
Accessibility is not a layer on top—it’s part of the design from the beginning.
Small improvements (like button spacing and contrast) significantly impact user trust and safety.
